content: The system of equations $4 x+6 y=5$ and $8 x+12 y=10$ has
explanation:
Notice that the second equation is just twice the first:

First equation:

$4x + 6y = 5$

Multiply both sides by 2:

$8x + 12y = 10$

Since both equations represent the same line, there are infinitely many solutions.

Answer: Option B.

content: A line passes through $(-1,-3)$ and perpendicular to $x+6 y=5$. Its $x$ intercept is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & y+3=6(x+1) \\ & x \text { intercept } y=0 \\ & x=\frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: The angle between the line $x+y=3$ and the line joining the points $(1,1)$ and $(-3,4)$ is
explanation:
$\because y=-x+3 ; m_1=-1=\tan \theta_1$

and $m_2=\frac{y-1}{x-1}=\frac{4-1}{-3-1}$

$$\frac{y-1}{x-1}=\frac{3}{-4} ; m_2=\frac{3}{-4}=\tan \theta_2$$

Angle between two lines are

$$\begin{aligned} & =\tan \left|\theta_2-\theta_1\right|=\frac{\tan \theta_2-\tan \theta_1}{1+\tan \theta_1 \cdot \tan \theta_2}=\frac{m_2-m_1}{1+m_1 m_2} \\ & =\frac{\frac{-3}{4}+1}{1+\frac{3}{4}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{4}{7} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $\left|\theta_2-\theta_1\right|=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{7}\right)$

content: A line passes through $$(2,2)$$ and is perpendicular to the line $$3 x+y=3$$. Its $$y$$-intercept is
explanation:
Equation of the line passing through $$(2,2)$$ and perpendicular to $$3 x+y=3$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & (x-2)-3(y-2)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & x-3 y=-4 \end{array}$$

$$\therefore y$$ intercept of this line is $$\frac{4}{3}$$.

content: If the straight line $$2 x-3 y+17=0$$ is perpendicular to the line passing through the points $$(7,17)$$ and $$(15, \beta)$$, then $$\beta$$ equals
explanation:
Equation of line passing through $$(7,17)$$ and $$(15, \beta)$$ is

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} &(y-17) =\left(\frac{\beta-17}{15-7}\right)(x-7) \\ \Rightarrow & y-17 =\frac{(\beta-17)}{8}(x-7) \\ \Rightarrow & y =\left(\frac{\beta-17}{8}\right) x-\frac{7(\beta-17)}{8}+17 \end{array}$$

On comparing to $$y=m x+c$$, we get Slope, $$m_1=\frac{\beta-17}{8}$$

It is given that above line is perpendicular to

$$2 x-3 y+17=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad y=\frac{2}{3} x+\frac{17}{3}$$

$$\therefore$$ Slope, $$m_2=\frac{2}{3}$$

Condition for perpendicular lines, $$m_1 \cdot m_2=-1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{\beta-17}{8}\right) \times \frac{2}{3}=-1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \beta-17=-12 \Rightarrow \beta=5 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the vertices of a triangle are $$(-2,6),(3,-6)$$ and $$(1,5)$$, then the area of the triangle is
explanation:
Given vertices of triangle are $$(-2,6),(3,-6)$$ and $$(1,5)$$.

Area of triangle $$=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll}x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1\end{array}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 6 & 1 \\ 3 & -6 & 1 \\ 1 & 5 & 1 \end{array}\right|=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -5 & 12 & 0 \\ 2 & -11 & 0 \\ 1 & 5 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ & {\left[R_1 \leftrightarrow R_1-R_2, R_2 \leftrightarrow R_2-R_3\right]} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}[1(55-24)]=\frac{31}{2}=15.5 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of straight line which passes through the point $$\left(a \cos ^3 \theta, a \sin ^3 \theta\right)$$ and perpendicular to $$x \sec \theta+y \operatorname{cosec} \theta=a$$ is
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Point }\left(x_1, y_1\right)=\left(a \cos ^3 \theta, a \sin ^3 \theta\right) \\ & \text { Other line } \Rightarrow x \sec \theta+y \operatorname{cosec} \theta=a \\ & \qquad y=-\frac{x \sec \theta}{\operatorname{cosec} \theta}+\frac{a}{\operatorname{cosec} \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=\left(-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right) x+a \sin \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=m_1 x+c \\ & \therefore \quad m_1=-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of required line

$$\Rightarrow\left(y-y_1\right)=m\left(x-x_1\right)$$

$$\text { where } m=\frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}\left[\because m_1 \cdot m=-1, m=-\frac{1}{m_1}\right. \left.=-\frac{1}{\left(-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right)}=\frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(y-a \sin ^3 \theta\right)=\frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}\left(x-a \cos ^3 \theta\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y \sin \theta-a \sin ^4 \theta=x \cos \theta-a \cos ^4 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta=a\left(\cos ^4 \theta-\sin ^4 \theta\right) \\ & \quad=a\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)\left(\cos ^2 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta\right) \\ & \quad=a \cdot(1) \cdot(\cos 2 \theta)=a \cos 2 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta=a \cos 2 \theta \end{aligned}$$

content: The two lines $$l x+m y=n$$ and $$l^{\prime} x+m^{\prime} y=n^{\prime}$$ are perpendicular if
explanation:
Given lines

$$l x+m y=n$$ and $$l x+m^{\prime} y=n^{\prime}$$

Slope of line $$l x+m y-n=0$$ is $$-l / m$$ and slope of line $$l x+m^{\prime} y-n^{\prime}=0$$ is $$-l^{\prime} / m^{\prime}$$ lines are perpendicular

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad\left(\frac{-l}{m}\right)\left(\frac{-l^{\prime}}{m^{\prime}}\right) & =-1 \\ ll^{\prime}+m m^{\prime} & =0 \end{aligned}$$

content: A line cuts off equal intercepts on the co-ordinate axes. The angle made by this line with the positive direction of $$X$$-axis is
explanation:
Equation of line which cuts off equal intercepts on co-ordinate axes,

$$\begin{gathered} x+y=a \Rightarrow y=-x+a \\ \text { Here, } \tan \theta=-1=\tan 135^{\circ} \Rightarrow \theta=135^{\circ} \end{gathered}$$

content: The equation of the line parallel to the line $3 x-4 y+2=0$ and passing through $(-2,3)$ is
explanation:
To find the equation of a line parallel to the given line $ 3x - 4y + 2 = 0 $ that passes through the point $(-2, 3)$, follow these steps:

Identify the form of the parallel line:

A line parallel to $ 3x - 4y + 2 = 0 $ will have the same coefficients for $ x $ and $ y $. Thus, the equation of the line we are looking for can be written as:

$ 3x - 4y = \lambda $

Determine $\lambda$ using the given point:

Since the line passes through the point $(-2, 3)$, substitute these coordinates into the equation:

$ 3(-2) - 4(3) = \lambda $

Calculate $\lambda$:

$ -6 - 12 = \lambda \implies \lambda = -18 $

Write the equation of the line:

Substitute $\lambda = -18$ back into the equation of the line:

$ 3x - 4y = -18 $

Express the equation in standard form:

Rewrite the equation as:

$ 3x - 4y + 18 = 0 $

Therefore, the equation of the line parallel to the given line and passing through the point $(-2, 3)$ is $ 3x - 4y + 18 = 0 $.

content: Equation of line passing through the point $(1,2)$ and perpendicular to the line $y=3 x-1$
explanation:
Given the line equation:

$ y = 3x - 1 $

The slope of this line is $ 3 $.

To find the equation of a line perpendicular to this, we need the negative reciprocal of the given line's slope. Thus, the slope of the perpendicular line is:

$ -\frac{1}{3} $

The equation of a line with this slope that passes through the point $(1, 2)$ can be set up using the point-slope form:

$ y - 2 = -\frac{1}{3}(x - 1) $

Expanding this equation, we get:

$ 3(y - 2) = -(x - 1) $

Which simplifies to:

$ 3y - 6 = -x + 1 $

Rearranging terms, we arrive at:

$ x + 3y - 7 = 0 $

This is the equation of the line that passes through the point $(1, 2)$ and is perpendicular to $ y = 3x - 1 $.
